introSection:
Thunder Road was a Philadelphia Toboggan Coasters wooden roller coaster located at Carowinds in Charlotte, North Carolina, USA. It was a twin roller coaster, featuring two individual tracks that ran parallel each other. The ride was designed by John C. Allen and Curtis D. Summers.

The coaster was built across the Carolinas state line. The coaster began in South Carolina and then crossed into North Carolina.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
On January 22, 1975, it was announced that a $2 million expansion was underway, which included a racing wooden roller coaster. The coaster's name Thunder Road was revealed in February 1976.

The ride originally featured automobile-themed trains that originated from the Jetstream at Riverview Park in Chicago, Illinois, USA. The trains were replaced by 1987.

The trains on one of the two tracks ran in reverse from 1996 through 2008.

On May 23, 2015, Carowinds announced that Thunder Road would be removed. It closed on July 26, 2015 and was replaced by the Carolina Harbor waterpark expansion. The ride's parts were reused on Racer at Kings Island and Racer 75 at Kings Dominion. In 2019, Copperhead Strike opened, with one turn occupying the former plot of Thunder Road.

sections.title: Design
sections.content: The design of the ride was based upon Rebel Yell, another wooden racing coaster at Kings Dominion in Doswell, Virginia, USA.

sections.title: Incident
sections.content: On April 5, 1999, a sensor failure caused a collision between a train and an empty train in the station. Sixteen riders were onboard, seven of whom were injured. The roller coaster reopened on April 9 following a replacement of all sensors.

sections.title: External links
sections.content: * Thunder Road on the parks website

fullText:
Thunder Road was a Philadelphia Toboggan Coasters wooden roller coaster located at Carowinds in Charlotte, North Carolina, USA. It was a twin roller coaster, featuring two individual tracks that ran parallel each other. The ride was designed by John C. Allen and Curtis D. Summers.

The coaster was built across the Carolinas state line. The coaster began in South Carolina and then crossed into North Carolina.

On January 22, 1975, it was announced that a $2 million expansion was underway, which included a racing wooden roller coaster. The coaster's name Thunder Road was revealed in February 1976.

The ride originally featured automobile-themed trains that originated from the Jetstream at Riverview Park in Chicago, Illinois, USA. The trains were replaced by 1987.

The trains on one of the two tracks ran in reverse from 1996 through 2008.

On May 23, 2015, Carowinds announced that Thunder Road would be removed. It closed on July 26, 2015 and was replaced by the Carolina Harbor waterpark expansion. The ride's parts were reused on Racer at Kings Island and Racer 75 at Kings Dominion. In 2019, Copperhead Strike opened, with one turn occupying the former plot of Thunder Road.

The design of the ride was based upon Rebel Yell, another wooden racing coaster at Kings Dominion in Doswell, Virginia, USA.



On April 5, 1999, a sensor failure caused a collision between a train and an empty train in the station. Sixteen riders were onboard, seven of whom were injured. The roller coaster reopened on April 9 following a replacement of all sensors.




 * Thunder Road on the parks website
